original_hypothesis.title: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount allows any connector token; a malicious connector token can grief/DoS claims by reverting on transfer (to veNFT owner) during getReward
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount is permissionless and accepts any token that is either already a bribeToken or passes tokenHandler.isConnector(). If a token is (or becomes) a connector, a malicious/non-standard ERC20 can be added as a bribe reward token. Later, during Bribe.getReward(), transfers are executed to the veNFT owner for each token requested; if the token reverts or behaves pathologically, it can cause getReward to revert, blocking the entire bribe claim transaction for that bribe (and potentially discouraging claims).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an attacker-controlled on-chain grief vector once a problematic token is classified as connector (or if a non-standard token is mistakenly allowed). It can be exploited without mempool/MEV assumptions and can materially disrupt user reward collection UX and operations (support load, forced token filtering in UIs).
deployment_assumptions: TokenHandler connector set includes at least one token with non-standard/reverting transfer semantics, OR connector governance can be influenced to include such a token
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No on-chain TokenHandler implementation provided in scope; connector policy and enforcement cannot be verified here.

proof_of_signal.pattern: notifyRewardAmount: require(_isRewardToken(_rewardsToken)); ... bribeTokens.push(_rewardsToken); safeTransferFrom(...); tokenRewardsPerEpoch[...] += reward

preconditions: Reward token qualifies via isBribeToken or tokenHandler.isConnector(token)==true
Attacker can fund notifyRewardAmount with that token
Claim path includes the problematic token in the tokens[] passed to getReward (via GaugeManager.claimBribes caller input)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Permissionless bribe token onboarding via connector designation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Connector list governance/ops determines blast radius
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any epoch after malicious token is funded into bribe

operational_questions: Does front-end/backend auto-include all bribeTokens in claim calls (making forced reverts more likely)?
Can users safely exclude the problematic token from claim requests to avoid reverting?
Does tokenHandler have safeguards against non-standard ERC20s?
validation_requirements: Test getReward with a reverting ERC20 in bribeTokens
Assess whether partial claiming is feasible in client workflows
Review connector governance and token vetting procedures

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Get a problematic token added as connector (or use an existing one)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call bribe.notifyRewardAmount(problemToken, amount) for targeted bribe
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Observe claim failures when users/automation attempt to claim including that token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sustain by topping up small amounts each epoch to keep token present

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Bribe claiming transactions revert; user rewards effectively stuck unless token filtered
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires UI/backend filtering and/or connector removal; funds may remain trapped
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Reward claiming, support operations, incentives reliability

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None if connector already exists; otherwise depends on connector governance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: New bribeTokens entries that are connectors; repeated small notifyRewardAmount deposits
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spike in reverted claim transactions for specific bribes/tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same attacker address funding nuisance tokens across many bribes

validation_result.mechanism: Permissionless bribe token pollution via connector tokens
validation_result.impact_summary: Any connector token can be permanently added to a Bribe's bribeTokens[] via the permissionless notifyRewardAmount(). If the token reverts on transfer (e.g., blocklisted address, non-standard ERC20), it disrupts claims that include that token and permanently pollutes the bribeTokens array with no removal mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies a real code deficiency in the Bribe contract's token onboarding logic. notifyRewardAmount is permissionless and accepts any token that either is already a bribeToken or passes tokenHandler.isConnector(). Once a token is added, it is permanently stored in bribeTokens[] with no removal function. If such a token has reverting transfer semantics (e.g., blacklist, pausable, fee-on-transfer issues), it can cause safeTransfer calls to revert in getReward when included in the claim tokens array.

However, the severity is partially mitigated: getReward() takes a caller-provided tokens[] array via GaugeManager.claimBribes(), meaning users CAN exclude the problematic token from their claims. The DoS is NOT total - users who manually filter can still claim other token rewards. The main impact is on automated systems, frontends that auto-include all bribeTokens, and API contracts (veNFTAPIV1, RewardAPI, AlgebraPoolAPI) that iterate over all bribeTokens and may revert.

The defect is the combination of: (1) permissionless notifyRewardAmount for connector tokens, (2) permanent and irreversible addition to bribeTokens[], and (3) no governance mechanism to remove problematic tokens from a Bribe.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies Bribes.sol lines containing notifyRewardAmount. The _isRewardToken check at line relies on tokenHandler.isConnector() for non-bribe tokens. getReward iterates over user-provided tokens and calls safeTransfer which will revert on non-standard tokens. The bribeTokens array has push operations but no pop/removal mechanism anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Any address that passes tokenHandler.isConnector() is accepted as a valid reward token, with no additional vetting. This allows any connector token (including potentially non-standard ones) to be added to bribeTokens permanently.

Permissionless function that permanently adds connector tokens to bribeTokens[] with no way to remove them. Anyone can call this with any connector token and a small amount to pollute the bribeTokens array.

If a problematic token is included in the tokens[] array and _reward > 0, safeTransfer will revert, causing the entire transaction to fail. Mitigated by the fact that tokens[] is user-controlled via claimBribes, so users can exclude problematic tokens.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker identifies a connector token with non-standard transfer behavior (reverting, blocklist, etc.)
2. Attacker calls Bribe.notifyRewardAmount(connectorToken, smallAmount) on target bribe
4. Token is permanently added: isBribeToken[connectorToken] = true; bribeTokens.push(connectorToken)
5. safeTransferFrom pulls the small amount from attacker
6. Later, when user/frontend calls GaugeManager.claimBribes including the malicious token in _tokens array
7. IBribe.getReward iterates tokens, calls earned() which returns > 0 for the malicious token
8. IERC20(maliciousToken).safeTransfer(_owner, _reward) reverts, blocking the entire claim tx
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: notifyRewardAmount (permissionless, connector token accepted) → bribeTokens.push (permanent, no removal) → getReward → safeTransfer (reverts on non-standard token). The attacker entry is notifyRewardAmount which has no access control beyond _isRewardToken check. The bribeTokens array is append-only with no delete/removal function in the entire Bribe contract.

deployment_assumptions_checked: TokenHandler connector set includes at least one token with non-standard/reverting transfer semantics
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure the TokenHandler connector set. The ITokenHandler interface is defined but no implementation is in scope. The connector token list governance/policy cannot be verified from the provided code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible if a connector token with non-standard transfer behavior exists. The attacker calls notifyRewardAmount with a small amount of that token on any target bribe. The token is permanently added to bribeTokens[]. The DoS is partial: users who include the problematic token in their claim tokens[] will have reverted transactions. Users who manually exclude it can still claim other rewards. API contracts (veNFTAPIV1._addExternalBribeRewards, AlgebraPoolAPI._getNextEpochRewards, RewardAPI._getNextEpochRewards) iterate over all bribeTokens and may also be affected if they call earned() or symbol()/decimals() on the malicious token.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH. The core defect is real: permissionless, permanent token addition with no removal mechanism. However, the DoS is not complete because getReward takes user-selected tokens. Impact includes: (1) permanent pollution of bribeTokens[] array, (2) frontend/API disruption when iterating all bribe tokens, (3) user confusion and support overhead, (4) rewards in the problematic token are effectively stuck. The defect is a missing safeguard (no removal mechanism, no governance override for bribe tokens) combined with overly permissive token acceptance.
recommended_mitigations: Add a governance-controlled function to remove tokens from bribeTokens[] and reset isBribeToken mapping
Add an access control check on notifyRewardAmount (e.g., require gauge or BRIBE_ADMIN role for adding new tokens)
Add a max cap on the number of bribeTokens per bribe contract
Use try/catch around safeTransfer in getReward to skip failing tokens rather than reverting the entire transaction
Add a token blacklist mechanism in the Bribe contract to prevent specific tokens from being claimed
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock TokenHandler with isConnector returning true for a malicious ERC20. 2. Deploy a malicious ERC20 that reverts on transfer to specific addresses. 3. Call notifyRewardAmount on target bribe with malicious token. 4. Verify token is permanently in bribeTokens[]. 5. Attempt getReward including the malicious token - verify revert. 6. Attempt getReward excluding the malicious token - verify success.
uncertainties: TokenHandler implementation and connector token governance process not in scope - cannot verify what tokens are/can be connectors
Frontend behavior when encountering unknown bribe tokens - may or may not auto-include all tokens
Whether any existing connector tokens have non-standard transfer behavior
additional_testing_needed: Review TokenHandler implementation to determine connector token vetting process
Test API contracts (veNFTAPIV1, RewardAPI, AlgebraPoolAPI) behavior when iterating over bribeTokens that include a reverting token
Test earned() calculation with a token that has no real deposits but was added via notifyRewardAmount with dust amount
Verify whether SafeERC20.safeTransfer handles all edge cases (return false, revert, no return data) for the specific Solidity version
